comments.post_body:
Not many of you may know but I spent a lot of my career in the pathology laboratories doing software engineering.

I have often thought how AI will revolutionize patient care, especially in terms of diagnostic services such as radiology and pathology. So it was exciting to see new research from my current employer Microsoft and my former, the UW, rolling research into exactly this, using AI to improve cancer outcomes.

Thinking of the good old days when Rodney Schmidt and I were trying "change the world" with software!
comments.context_summary: The commenter is sharing their background in software engineering in pathology laboratories and expressing excitement about new research from Microsoft and the University of Washington that uses AI to improve cancer outcomes. They are responding to their own post about the potential of AI to revolutionize patient care, particularly in diagnostic services like radiology and pathology.

comments.comment_body:
Stefan de Kooter - Great Question!

For direct VNet-to-VNet connectivity, we recommend Vnet Peering or Azure Virtual Network Manager Meshes.
 
In this case, Routing Appliance is primarily intended for customers who already rely on NVAs due to their existing network topologies. These customers often find themselves limited by throughput bottlenecks and are looking for a way to scale up to multiple tbps bandwidth. The current implementation supports routing (via UDRs), access control (via NSGs) and Private Links.
 
As we move towards general availability (GA), we will be adding additional features to support broader networking use cases. Also remember this is still a public preview and we are looking to get feedback from everyone on the product.
comments.post_body:
Virtual Network Routing Appliance ("Routing Appliance") Public Preview!

During Ignite, I posted about exciting developments in building a high-performance, native appliance in Azure that will help customers achieve low-latency, high-throughput routing without the need of a Network Virtual Appliance (NVA). Today I'm happy to announce that we have released a public preview of this feature. I hope you get a chance to try it out and we are looking to build many more exciting capabilities on top of this new technology.

NVAs have been the backbone of complex Azure networking topologies where customers needed to route traffic through a central point. While there are still many use cases for NVAs, such as advanced firewalls, for many scenarios NVAs just added complexity but were required to provide a route point.  NVAs require the deployment of a virtual machine, route traffic through the guest OS, require the additional overhead of OS management and can create a bottleneck in terms of performance.

Today, with the announcement of the Routing Appliance you get configurable "Azure native" route point with networking speeds up to 200Gbps.  You get the management ease of any Azure resource. No need to deploy or configure an operating system. With just a few clicks and an Azure resource deployment, you have a high scale route enabled appliance with native support for Network Security Groups (NSGs) and User Defined Routes (UDRs) giving you easy traffic filtering and routing.

Read More: https://lnkd.in/gWqdVEjv

Thanks to Ashish Bhargava, Mario Lopez, Mohamed Hassan, Jackson Crewe, Nitin Goel, Pan Wang, Anshu Verma, Arjun Patel, Rishabh Tewari, Neeraj Motwani, Avijit Gupta, Damini Singal, Michal Zygmunt, Deepak Bansal

#azure #azurenetworking #microsoft
comments.context_summary: The post announces the public preview of the Virtual Network Routing Appliance, a native Azure appliance that enables low-latency, high-throughput routing without the need for a Network Virtual Appliance (NVA). This new technology aims to simplify Azure networking topologies and provide high-performance routing capabilities with ease of management.

comments.post_body:
I had a wonderful time last evening guest lecturing at the University of Washington about "Databases in the AI era".

A huge thank you to Kevin Fleming for inviting me and facilitating such a great session. 

We had a fantastic deep dive into the evolving landscape of databases and the role AI plays in modern data architecture. It is always a privilege to share my journey as an AI Product Manager and provide a peek behind the curtain at how we build at enterprise scale. The energy in the room was contagious, and the questions from the students regarding the future of data and Agentic AI were incredibly sharp. The future of tech is in good hands!

Huskies, thanks for having me. 🐺💜
comments.context_summary: The commenter had a great experience guest lecturing at the University of Washington about databases in the AI era, and appreciated the opportunity to share their knowledge with students. They expressed gratitude to the invitation and facilitation by Kevin Fleming, and were impressed by the students' sharp questions about the future of data and AI.

comments.post_body:
As I said in a previous post, today is exciting to have 2 great announcements.  This is the second one, where I am so excited to see my sibling team release Network Security Perimeter (NSP) into General Availability! I have had the great privilege to work with this amazing team as they have built a complimentary feature to Azure Virtual Network Manager in the PaaS space.  While not my direct team or product, NSP team is my closest peer and I've had the great opportunity to work with them on their design, share code, and build great ideas together.  It's a great day to celebrate their achievement and bringing an amazing product to the Azure Portfolio.

Network security perimeter allows organizations to define a logical network isolation boundary for PaaS resources (for example, Azure Storage account and SQL Database server) that are deployed outside your organization’s virtual networks. It restricts public network access to PaaS resources within the perimeter; access can be exempted by using explicit access rules for public inbound and outbound.  

Features of a network security perimeter include:  
* Resource to resource access communication within perimeter members, preventing data exfiltration to non-authorized destinations.  
* External public access management with explicit rules for PaaS resources associated with the perimeter.  
* Access logs for audit and compliance.  
* Unified experience across PaaS resources. 

Learn more about NSP: https://lnkd.in/gY-CvgY6

One great example of using NSP is with Azure Monitor:
https://lnkd.in/gJW6mwc4
comments.context_summary: The commenter is congratulating their sibling team on the general availability of Network Security Perimeter (NSP), a feature that provides logical network isolation for PaaS resources, and is celebrating their achievement in bringing this product to the Azure portfolio. The commenter had the opportunity to work with the NSP team on design and development, and is excited about the product's features and potential use cases.

comments.post_body:
Today I have two exciting announcements.  Let's get into the first one!

We are excited to announce that a public preview of supporting up 5,000 virtual networks in an Azure Virtual Network Manager mesh connectivity. A mesh topology creates bi‑directional connectivity between every virtual network in a mesh connectivity configuration, so selected Virtual networks communicate directly, eliminating manual peerings, avoiding extra hops, and delivering low‑latency traffic flows under a unified control plane.

The original limit was 250 virtual networks in a mesh with a maximum of 1000 with a support request.  The enablement to 5,000 will allow a huge increase in scale for our customers!

Announcement: https://lnkd.in/gQk5qcXW
comments.context_summary: The post announces a public preview of Azure Virtual Network Manager's ability to support up to 5,000 virtual networks in a mesh connectivity configuration, significantly increasing the original limit of 250 virtual networks. This enhancement aims to provide a huge increase in scale for customers, allowing for more efficient and low-latency traffic flows.

comments.post_body:
📣Finally general availability of Route-Maps for Azure virtual WAN 🥳

If you have not tried it out, then it’s now time 😁

Route-maps is a feature that gives you the ability to control route advertisements and routing for Virtual WAN virtual hubs. 

Like summarize routes, control routes entering/leaving vHub, control routing decisions by modifying BGP attributes like AS-Path and tag routes using BGP communities. 

Read about the announcement here: https://lnkd.in/d9nV4Gkz
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to the announcement of the general availability of Route-Maps for Azure virtual WAN, a feature that allows control over route advertisements and routing for Virtual WAN virtual hubs. The commenter is likely excited about the feature's capabilities, such as summarizing routes and controlling routing decisions.

comments.comment_body: Jannick Oeben thanks for the feedback. I'll take this back to our business team.
comments.post_body:
One of the most asked for features by customers for Azure Virtual Network Manager (AvNM) isn't a feature at all! It was to change our billing model. 

Previously, AvNM billed based on subscriptions with managed virtual networks. However, this model didn't align with Azure best practices that recommend having a larger number of subscriptions. For many customers, this blocked adoption.

Now, we bill directly based on the virtual networks managed. This model of billing aligns with the actual resources under management and gives a more accurate bill for our customers. 

I'm pleased to announce that all Azure public regions now have this new billing model and all future network managers will be billed based on virtual networks.

Aidan Finn, I know you have been a big champion of this and special thanks for your ongoing support and feedback!
comments.context_summary: The Azure Virtual Network Manager has changed its billing model from subscription-based to virtual network-based, aligning with Azure best practices and providing more accurate billing for customers. This change is now available in all Azure public regions, addressing a long-requested feature by customers.

comments.post_body:
IP Address Management (IPAM)! 

It's something that I remember doing 20 years ago in an Excel spreadsheet. And many companies today still fall back to this tried-and-true method for managing their IP address allocations. 

However, this week I'm really excited to announce that Azure Virtual Network Manager IPAM is in public preview.

Now customers can create IP address pools, child pools to help organize, and then allocate Azure Virtual Networks directly from these address pools. You also can keep track of all your on-premises allocations!  No more accidental overlapping of IP address spaces between virtual networks or with on-prem systems. 

Create a Network Manager ⇨ Create your Pool ⇨ Ready to Go
Create a Virtual Network ⇨ Now pick an allocation pool for your IP space
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to an announcement about Azure Virtual Network Manager IPAM being in public preview, which allows for easier management of IP address allocations. The new feature aims to replace traditional methods, such as using Excel spreadsheets, by providing a more organized and efficient way to manage IP addresses.

comments.comment_body: Congrats! Great job!
comments.post_body:
🌟 Grateful and Honored! 🌟

I am beyond thrilled to share that I have been recognized with the Young Achiever Alumnus Award by my alma mater, St. Vincent Pallotti College of Engineering.

When I first heard about my nomination, I was already overwhelmed with gratitude—being considered among such talented individuals was an achievement in itself. Winning this award feels surreal and is a reminder of the incredible journey I’ve been on, from those classroom benches to where I stand today.

This recognition celebrates the work I’ve done in the field of Artificial Intelligence and the mentorship program I was privileged to run during college. Guiding and empowering students to pursue their dreams has been one of the most rewarding experiences of my life.

To the students of the new batch: dream big, embrace every challenge, and never stop learning. You have the power to create your own inspiring story!

#Gratitude #YoungAchiever #ArtificialIntelligence #Mentorship #AlmaMater #AlumniAward #Motivation #ProudMoment #AI #ArtificialIntelligence
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about being awarded the Young Achiever Alumnus Award for their work in Artificial Intelligence and a mentorship program, and is expressing gratitude and sharing motivational words for students. The award winner is reflecting on their journey and the impact of their work, feeling honored and surreal about the recognition.

comments.comment_body: Peet van de Sande it is more than that. We are not just creating peerings between all your Vnets but actually introduced a new concept in the networking stack to enable directly creating meshes.
comments.post_body:
A great day for Connectivity in Azure!  After years of effort, I'm pleased to announce that we have released Mesh networks and Hub/Spoke with Transitive connective to general availability.

Customers can now connect multiple virtual networks together with ease. In the past you would have to create virtual peerings for every connection, route tables, suffer additional hops and latency.  Now, with Azure Virtual Network Manager, in a matter of minutes you can connect hundreds of virtual networks together with no additional hops, incredibly low latency, and without the need to create and manage route tables.

I'm very proud of all the work my team has done to bring this product to market as we continue to build the best cloud network in the world.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to the announcement of the general availability of Mesh networks and Hub/Spoke with Transitive connectivity in Azure, which allows customers to easily connect multiple virtual networks together with low latency. This new feature simplifies network management and reduces the need for manual configuration.

comments.post_body:
Excited to announce DivUp! 🎉 I made an app that makes splitting receipts a breeze while ensuring privacy and ease of use. Add your friends, scan your receipt, split the items, and bam—you get a nicely split receipt to easily share with friends! Check out the video to see it in action. 

iOS app: https://lnkd.in/gdBq5x5r

Privacy is a top priority with DivUp. Your receipt images and data are stored locally on your device, never on external servers. DivUp uses your phone's onboard OCR and a unique algorithm to extract items from the receipt, ensuring your data remains secure and private.

DivUp is currently free! I would love to get your feedback to make it even better. Try it out and let me know what you think!

#DivUp #Fintech #Privacy #EaseOfUse
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to the announcement of DivUp, a new app that simplifies splitting receipts while prioritizing user privacy and ease of use. The app's creator is seeking feedback on the free iOS app to further improve its functionality.

comments.post_body: Excited to see Virtual Network Verifier (or VNV as we call it) get launched to public preview!! One of the biggest challenges for customers is being able to verify traffic flows within their complex configurations.  With VNV customers can now very quickly answer questions like "Why can't my VM reach my database?"  Please take a moment to go try it for yourself.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to the launch of Virtual Network Verifier (VNV) to public preview, which helps customers verify traffic flows in complex configurations. They are excited about the potential of VNV to quickly resolve issues, such as connectivity problems between virtual machines and databases.

comments.comment_body: Anders Spælling - Let's chat.  Can you send me or Andrea Michael a message about your concerns and we can see how to make the product available to a larger audience.
comments.post_body:
A great day for Connectivity in Azure!  After years of effort, I'm pleased to announce that we have released Mesh networks and Hub/Spoke with Transitive connective to general availability.

Customers can now connect multiple virtual networks together with ease. In the past you would have to create virtual peerings for every connection, route tables, suffer additional hops and latency.  Now, with Azure Virtual Network Manager, in a matter of minutes you can connect hundreds of virtual networks together with no additional hops, incredibly low latency, and without the need to create and manage route tables.

I'm very proud of all the work my team has done to bring this product to market as we continue to build the best cloud network in the world.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to the announcement of the general availability of Mesh networks and Hub/Spoke with Transitive connectivity in Azure, which allows customers to easily connect multiple virtual networks together with low latency. This new feature simplifies the process of connecting virtual networks, eliminating the need for manual peerings and route table management.

comments.comment_body: Want to add a thank you to Microsoft Research as well for partnering on this project with us!  Ashish Bhargava and Karthick J..
comments.post_body:
I'm excited to announce my amazing team has brought UDR management to public preview!  The capability to manage custom route tables has been a major ask from customers and it's great to see this capability being brought to Azure.

Give it a try and look for a general availability release soon.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to the announcement of UDR management reaching public preview in Azure, a highly requested feature by customers. They express excitement about the development and encourage others to try it out.

comments.comment_body: "Microsoft's plans to protect its networks include 100% network isolation and segmentation, while its efforts to secure engineering systems will focus on — among other things" <--  hey that's the team I used to lead for Azure. 😁
comments.post_body:
Typical Microsoft - this is why their software is always so mediocre.

I get the need for some good PR - but this spineless, reactionary approach to value creation isn’t how you get the job done.

Either you create a vision for your product that includes the appropriate security stance or you don’t.

Bribing executives to handle it in a blanket way is trying to get the reward without putting in the effort.

I mean what do you do that applies across the board, to all products, say, “don’t embarrass us and you get a $2MM bonus every year”?
comments.context_summary: The commenter is criticizing Microsoft's approach to value creation, calling it 'spineless' and 'reactionary', and believes the company should create a clear vision for its products instead of relying on blanket solutions. They think Microsoft's method of incentivizing executives with bonuses is ineffective and lacks real effort.

They believe AVNM is technically improving, but has room for further development.

comments.post_body:
☁️ Azure Virtual Network Manager (AVNM) Fridays: Connectivity ☁️
I’m excited to dive into a series on a fantastic product I’ve been working on – Azure Virtual Network Manager. Every week, I’ll share a piece of AVNM’s magic – after a brief hiatus, we’re now diving into connectivity configurations!
Check out my previous AVNM Fridays posts covering the basics and security admin rules:
🌐https://lnkd.in/g3qUWVty 
🔒https://lnkd.in/g-4GksSZ 

🤔 What is an AVNM connectivity configuration?
In AVNM, connectivity configurations are how you build and manage your network topologies at scale. In a connectivity config, you specify your desired network topology, which network groups should be a part of that topology, and some additional options (like establishing direct connectivity among the members of a network group). Then you can deploy your connectivity configs to whichever regions you want! AVNM can even clean up your old existing peerings after config deployment.
For your config’s topology, you can choose between mesh, hub and spoke, and hub and spoke with direct connectivity (think mini-mesh among each network groups’ respective members). AVNM also provides a visualization tool from Azure Resource Topology so you can see the connectivity your config is creating among network groups and their member VNets!

💼 Use cases
So, what can I do with AVNM’s connectivity configurations?
o Connect VNets across subscriptions and regions in just a few clicks.
o Leverage central infrastructure services in a hub VNet with other VNets.
--> Establish direct connectivity among spoke VNets to reduce latency.
--> For example, you can create a hub and spoke topology with all of your production and test VNets connected to the same hub VNet, and enable direct connectivity among only your production VNets – all in the same config. 
o Automatically maintain connectivity at scale, even with the addition of new network resources.
--> Defining your network groups’ membership with Azure Policy removes the manual overhead of ensuring your new and existing VNets are connected as intended.

💡Where can I learn more?
🔗AVNM public docs on connectivity configs: https://lnkd.in/gSujFH6N 
🔗Wired For Hybrid deep-dive: https://lnkd.in/gZQ2-9XF
🔗Azure blog on AVNM use cases: https://lnkd.in/g7V5_kpG

🚀Thanks for tuning in to AVNM Friday! Drop your Qs and feedback in the comments, and check back for more AVNM insights + tips to elevate your cloud game 🚀
🟥🟩🟦🟨

#AVNM #azure #AzureVirtualNetworkManager #cloud #cloudjourney #connectivity #hubandspoke #mesh #networks #networkconnectivity #networkmanagement #networksolutions #networktopology #peerings
comments.context_summary: The post introduces Azure Virtual Network Manager's (AVNM) connectivity configurations, which enable users to build and manage network topologies at scale, and discusses various use cases, such as connecting VNets across subscriptions and regions, and establishing direct connectivity among spoke VNets. The commenter is responding to the introduction of AVNM's connectivity configurations and their applications.

comments.post_body:
☁️ Azure Virtual Network Manager (AVNM) Fridays: The Basics ☁️
I’m excited to dive into a series on a fantastic product I’ve been working on – Azure Virtual Network Manager. Every week, I’ll share a piece of AVNM’s magic – and today, we’ll start with the basics!

Whether you're a seasoned cloud pro or just starting your cloud journey, AVNM is a game-changer in managing your Azure network resources globally and at scale. Today, the AVNM service is generally available, along with its hub and spoke connectivity configuration and security admin configuration (in 30 regions and counting).

🤔 What is AVNM and how does it work?
Azure Virtual Network Manager is a centralized network management solution that helps you manage the connectivity and security of your network resources. Once you create your network manager resource, putting this into action only takes 3 main steps:
   1️⃣ Group your network resources however you see fit. You can even automatically group your resources by defining conditions.
   2️⃣ Create your connectivity and security admin configurations to achieve your desired topology and enforce organization-level policies.
   3️⃣ Deploy your configurations onto your network groups in any regions you want.

🔑 Key benefits
So, what makes AVNM stand out? 
   · 🌐Centralized controls --> simplified global management across regions and subscriptions.
   · ✨Automatic configuration application on your network resources --> connectivity maintenance and day-zero protection, even for new resources.
   · 🔐Enforced security rules across your organization’s new and existing network resources --> mitigate risk of changes or security holes.
   · 🌍Regional deployment of configuration changes --> safely test in regions, fix, & roll forward.

💡Where can I learn more?
🔗AVNM's most recent GA update: https://lnkd.in/gZnqwM4D
🔗AVNM public docs: https://lnkd.in/gsFHPEym
🔗Wired For Hybrid deep-dive: https://lnkd.in/gZQ2-9XF
🔗Azure blog on AVNM use cases: https://lnkd.in/g7V5_kpG
🔗AVNM product page: https://lnkd.in/gdFUFmx8

🚀Thanks for tuning in to AVNM Friday! Drop your Qs and feedback in the comments, and check back next week for more AVNM insights + tips to elevate your cloud game 🚀

🟥🟩🟦🟨

#AVNM #azure #AzureVirtualNetworkManager #cloud #cloudjourney  #networks #networksecurity #networksolutions #networkmanagement

comments.comment_body: This ratio is way too high unless you have a very focused and small project with excellent customer specs. And it tends to forget all the other aspects a leader brings to the group.  40 1x1s a week would be a nightmare and the leader would be unable to provide wisdom and true empathy to each employee along their career journey.  The best number I've seen is 7 to 8 per leader.
comments.post_body:
One leader, 40 developers.

That's the leadership ratio for a well-shaped, well-led software development team.

Set this ratio as your target, and then fix, as soon as humanly possible, anything that arises that might keep you from it.

#softwaredevelopment #leadershipcraftsmanship #team
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the ideal leadership ratio for a software development team, suggesting one leader for every 40 developers, and the commenter is responding directly to this proposal. The commenter's response is not provided, but they are likely offering their thoughts on the effectiveness of this leadership ratio.

comments.comment_body:
Ah yes - this is quite a common limiting belief - that the leader is there to dispense wisdom and to guide folks through their career. 

It assumes a mentor role for the leader that doesn’t need to exist, and very few software leaders can actually fulfill, anyway.

The leader is there to lead the development of software - this means setting up systems, ensuring the people are of the right caliber, and ensuring that the teams that the people form into have the skills to be and actually are autonomous. This is a full-time job, and one that only the leader can do.

Having a bunch of regular one-on-ones and pretending to be a mentor that you likely aren’t unless your team is all super junior, while it may be gratifying, isn’t leading.

Some strategic mentoring and individual value-add is important, don’t get me wrong - when it’s actually possible. But scheduling it super-regularly is not only self-deception, it’s a massive waste of time and a dereliction of duty.

They suggest a more ideal ratio is 7 to 8 developers per leader, allowing for more effective leadership and mentorship.

comments.comment_body: Kyle Rowland - I can agree that 1x1 without purpose is a waste.  But I also think a manager has a lot more roles than you outline. As such scaling to have a larger team is not a good solution.

comments.comment_body:
Yes - I’d agree that there are a lot of managers that find themselves doing things that aren’t focused on building the people system, and much of that even at the behest of their organizations.

I’m also not saying grow your team just for the sake of it - if your org only has/needs 5 engineers - don’t hire more just to meet a ratio.

Similarly - if you have 100 engineers and you pay for managers over every 5 people - you’re doing it wrong. :)

comments.comment_body:
You talk about hiring too many junior developers but there is a very small pool of talented seniors out there.  Secondly juniors don't become seniors without mentorship and guidance. My job is to ensure my team grows and my juniors become competent self-managing engineers.  I purposely give tasks that I could do in 2 days to a dev that will take weeks to complete so they can grow.

Secondly Devops builds better code in my opinion.  After waking up at 3am a few times due to a live site issue most devs quickly learn the importance of releasing well tested and quality code.
comments.post_body:
Forcing a software team to reach outside itself for any technical concern will limit the value the team is creating, and it will limit the organization’s ability to scale. 

Architecture Teams, “DevOps” Teams, Frontend/Backend Teams, DBA Teams - these are all ill-advised waste.  True concern about organizational value creation will put eliminating these albatross at the top of the to-do list.

A common root problem that can lead to their formation is simply hiring too junior software developers.  Another is mistakenly transferring the idea of economy of scale from an Industrial Age setting to an Information Age one.  You may need to remedy these dysfunctions before streamlining your team.

#softwaredevelopment #leadershipcraftsmanship #informationage

comments.comment_body:
I don't disagree with your first two points; the pool of qualified software developers is small, and junior developers need mentoring.  Both very true.  

Your third point regarding "devops" is a function of the first two - which is what I was trying to get across in the post - since folks are hiring teams that are too junior, all of their code is poorer than it could/should be, devops code included.

There's nothing inherent to an external team that leads to better code.  It's the fact that the engineers are more versed in what they are doing.
